data.title: Pornhub Ends USDT Support And Fully Migrates Creator Payouts To USDC Across Platform
data.description:
USDC Edges Out USDT As Pornhub Reshapes Creator Payments

Pornhub has changed how it pays its creators, replacing Tether’s USDT with Circle’s USDC across its payout system.

The shift affects one of the most heavily trafficked platforms on the internet, which processes earnings for creators operating in regions where traditional banking is often limited or unavailable.

The company says the change is aimed at making payouts “more reliable” while aligning with regulatory expectations that are increasingly shaping how global platforms choose payment rails.

Even Pornhub switching from USDT to USDC.

What’s going on? pic.twitter.com/aj3nxr0tD6

— Gracie Hartie ONLYF (@graciehartie) April 21, 2026

USDC Replaces USDT In Creator Payout System

The adult platform, which consistently ranks among the world’s top 15 websites with 4.26 billion visits in March 2026, confirmed that USDC is now the primary stablecoin for creator payments.

USDT has been removed from its payment options page, leaving USDC alongside Paxum, Verge, and Cosmo.

Creators were informed via email that they must update their payment details by 1 June 2026 to ensure uninterrupted payouts.

The message described USDC as “MiCA-compliant and regulated”, without referencing USDT directly.

Why Is Pornhub Prioritising Regulatory Alignment Now?

Pornhub’s decision reflects growing pressure on platforms operating across multiple jurisdictions to show clearer compliance with financial rules.

Unlike earlier years when speed and cost were the main concerns, regulatory clarity has become a deciding factor.

USDC, issued by Circle, is supported by monthly reserve disclosures and holds licensing recognition in markets such as France through its electronic money institution approval, helping it meet Europe’s MiCA framework requirements.

USDT, while still the largest stablecoin with a market capitalisation of around US$188 billion compared with USDC’s US$78 billion, does not carry the same formal regulatory approvals in several key regions.

$188 Billion.@tether's $USDT hit a new all-time high this month, up 188x from $1B seven years ago.

Data: @artemispic.twitter.com/jbQH4Q0Fc0

— Eco (@eco) April 22, 2026

This difference is becoming more relevant for companies that rely on banking partners and payment processors that now assess stablecoin risk more strictly.

From Banking Shutdown To Crypto Dependence And Back To Compliance Pressure

Pornhub first turned to USDT in 2020 after major payment providers including Visa, Mastercard, and PayPal severed ties with the platform.

At the time, Tether’s stablecoin offered a practical workaround: fast settlement, low fees, and access through the Tron network via TronLink integration.

That setup helped maintain global payouts in markets where banking access was inconsistent.

@Pornhub has added Tether’s #USDT stablecoin to its payout options for models. The payouts are set to be conducted using the @TronLinkWallet. #TRON#TRXhttps://t.co/JnlCIx5BRR

— TRON DAO (@trondao) January 26, 2020

However, that infrastructure partnership is no longer in use within its creator programme.

The latest move signals that the priorities have shifted again.

While crypto originally solved a banking access problem, compliance expectations are now shaping which crypto tools remain acceptable.

Is USDC Becoming The Preferred Stablecoin For Regulated Platforms?

Circle has positioned USDC as a compliance-first alternative, and that positioning is gaining traction with platforms under regulatory scrutiny.

The stablecoin’s reserve transparency and licensing framework have made it more attractive to businesses seeking stability with regulators and banking partners.

USDT still leads in liquidity and adoption, particularly in decentralised finance and emerging markets.

It continues to dominate trading flows and recently expanded its influence through capital deployments, including a US$127.5 million injection linked to a DeFi protocol recovery that reinforced its presence in crypto-native ecosystems.

🚨UPDATE: @tether will contribute up to $127.5 million to help @DriftProtocol recover from its recent exploit, as part of a broader $150 million recovery plan.

The protocol is also migrating its settlement layer from USDC to USDT as part of the restructuring effort. pic.twitter.com/1dx9W80Y58

— Cryptic (@Cryptic_Web3) April 18, 2026

However, the market is increasingly splitting into two operational lanes: USDT remaining strong in high-liquidity trading environments, and USDC gaining ground among platforms that depend on regulated financial access.

Pornhub’s decision places it firmly in the second category, highlighting how even non-traditional digital platforms are adjusting to a more compliance-driven financial landscape.

data.title: Leung Man-fung is still too conservative.
data.description:
DeepSeek is actively seeking funding. According to The Information, DeepSeek plans to raise at least $300 million at a valuation of at least $10 billion, marking its first time opening its doors to external capital since its inception. For over a year, DeepSeek has deliberately maintained a rare stance in the AI ​​industry: no fundraising, no commercialization, and no roadshows. The continuous influx of profits from its parent company, Magic Cube Quant, has allowed it to remain outside the realm of capital logic. This is scarce in the AI ​​sector and has thus earned it considerable positive reputation.

After all, DeepSeekIn the endIt is different from Kimi, Doubao and Qianwen

Back then, DeepSeek's competitor was GPT, but now, although only a little over a year has passed, its competitors have become Claude, Gemini, and Grok.Unfortunately, the performance of DeepSeek R1 and V3 has now been clearly suppressed by Claude and Gemini.Unfortunately, the performance of DeepSeek R1 and V3 has now been suppressed by Claude and Gemini. AI iterates rapidly, and external assessments of a company's current model's generational position subtly influence the decision of top researchers to stay or leave. Whether talent is willing to stay depends in part on their belief that there is still a significant technological battle to be fought here. Therefore, in addition to monetary incentives, DeepSeek must also produce products that surpass Claude and Gemini to prove that it still has enough "technological strength" to fight—this funding round has anchored a market valuation of $10 billion. The core function of this figure is twofold: first, to make the stock options held by employees real; and second, to demonstrate Deepseek's aggressive stance in technology, allowing Deepseek's research and development efforts to continue.

10 billionUS dollarsWhat kind of pricing is being given

So, is 10 billionUS dollarsexpensive?

It depends on the situation. ... One thing we must know is that, over the past year, DeepSeek has been a very laid-back project. To date, no systematic revenue stream has been found for Deepseek, and its API pricing is extremely low, with its ToB product line being even more so. The text is still in its early stages, almost like a side hustle for some big shots who don't care about making money; it's just for fun. Looking at Deepseek in comparison to Anthropic would be more interesting. This company also prioritizes technological research and has also produced the groundbreaking AI product Claude, but its valuation has already exceeded $380 billion. The underlying logic supporting Anthropic's high valuation is twofold: The overt logic is that Anthropic's commercialization is more aggressive than DeepSeek's. On the consumer side, memberships are selling like hotcakes. Relying on its strong model capabilities, it doesn't worry about sales and frequently resorts to "big company bullying customers," limiting token allocations one day and requiring real-name authentication the next, creating a pure seller's market. Its current annualized revenue has reached $14 billion. The underlying logic is this: Claude, as a top-tier model, has become a symbol of American technological strength, attracting investment from various international capital groups, including but not limited to Singapore's GIC, Blackstone, Goldman Sachs, Temasek, and Nvidia. In short, these international investors are more optimistic about American AI. The massive investment from international capital is not just a commercial bet, but also a strategic statement. This "leadership premium" accounts for a significant portion of Anthropic's valuation. Everyone should remember what happened last year—the explosive success of DeepSeek-R1 caused considerable ripples in the US tech and policy circles. As a representative of top US AI research institutions, Anthropic's strategic value was further amplified in this context. DeepSeek's $10 billion valuation follows a similar logic. Deepseek's core technological asset is its highly cost-effective training method, capable of training excellent models at extremely low cost. But more importantly, this methodology demonstrates a highly "dangerous" possibility to the world: it allows for the creation of impressive large-scale models without excessive reliance on Nvidia or significant investment. This signal is quite alarming. Because if we extrapolate further, we might see a very exciting scenario: perhaps in the near future, Deepseek, using its expertise in cost-effective training methods, will train open-source models on domestically produced GPUs with performance comparable to Claude's. If this scenario becomes a reality, it would be a "hellish picture" for Nvidia, Claude, and the international capital that has bet on them. Therefore, in Lao Ju's view, if we're only evaluating Deepseek's earning power, then a valuation of $10 billion is clearly too high. And if we're evaluating Deepseek's strategic importance, then this $10 billion valuation seems rather modest. It can only be said that Liang Wenfeng was ultimately too conservative.

data.title: Uzbekistan Opens Tax-Free Crypto Mining Zone—But Forces Profits Through State Banking System
data.description:
Uzbekistan is opening its doors to global crypto miners with sweeping tax incentives, but at the same time tightening control over where the money goes.

Under a new presidential decree, the country has launched the “Besqala Mining Valley” in the Karakalpakstan region—a state-backed mining zone designed to attract foreign investment by offering tax exemptions through 2035 and expanded operational flexibility.

UZBEKISTAN CREATES "BESQALA MINING VALLEY" SPECIAL CRYPTO MINING ZONE COVERING ALL OF KARAKALPAKSTAN

President Shavkat Mirziyoyev signed Presidential Decree PQ-143 on April 17, in force from April 20, creating the Besqala Mining Valley special mining zone covering the entire… pic.twitter.com/tluCrKIAOB

— BSCN (@BSCNews) April 22, 2026

But the incentives come with a critical condition: all proceeds from mining activities must flow through Uzbekistan’s domestic banking system.

The framework allows licensed operators to sell mined crypto assets on both local and international platforms, including through direct contracts. However, despite this global access, revenue repatriation remains strictly enforced, effectively anchoring profits within the national financial system.

Open Crypto Markets, Closed Capital Flows

The structure highlights a growing hybrid approach to crypto adoption, where governments seek to benefit from the industry’s upside while maintaining strict oversight of capital movement.

In Uzbekistan’s case, miners gain access to foreign liquidity and trading venues, but are required to channel earnings back into local accounts. The model reflects a broader attempt to balance investor appeal with financial sovereignty, ensuring that economic gains from mining activity remain within the country.

🇺🇿 Uzbekistan is launching a state-backed crypto mining zone with tax incentives to attract miners.

Firms can sell crypto abroad — but must repatriate proceeds through domestic banks.

A controlled push to tap into the global mining economy. pic.twitter.com/hCCjAeQTLp

— The Crypto Times (@CryptoTimes_io) April 22, 2026

At the same time, operators must pay a monthly fee equivalent to 1% of mining income to the zone’s governing body, reinforcing the state’s role in overseeing and monetizing the sector.

The new mining zone also marks a shift in Uzbekistan’s energy policy. Earlier regulations required crypto miners to rely exclusively on solar power, significantly limiting scalability. The updated framework now permits a mix of energy sources, including renewable, hydrogen, and grid electricity, though higher tariffs apply to grid usage.

What are the risks of Uzbekistan's full-scale transition to "green" energy

The visit of the Russian delegation led by the First Deputy Head of the Presidential Administration of Russia, Sergei Kirienko, to Uzbekistan on April 15, 2026, coincided with a period of record growth in… pic.twitter.com/H3fcvvxXhI

— 🇷🇺 WARGONZO -ULYANA STRIZH 🇷🇺 (@UlyanaStrizh) April 22, 2026

This move significantly broadens the appeal of the zone to international mining firms, many of which require consistent and scalable energy supply to operate efficiently.

A Broader Strategy to Attract Capital

The mining initiative forms part of a wider push to transform Karakalpakstan, a region identified by development agencies as economically underdeveloped. By combining tax incentives with regulatory clarity, Uzbekistan is positioning the area as a hub for emerging technologies, including crypto mining, artificial intelligence, and data infrastructure.

Previous initiatives have already introduced tax-free zones for AI and data center projects, with the government targeting over $1 billion in foreign investment by the end of the decade.

While Uzbekistan’s approach signals a clear openness to crypto innovation, it also underscores a key trend in emerging markets: the move toward controlled adoption.

By allowing global participation while restricting capital outflows, the country is effectively rewriting the rules of crypto mining—turning a traditionally decentralized industry into a state-aligned economic tool.

data.title: Crime Coin: The Most Profitable Business in the Crypto Industry
data.description:
Author: Vaidik Mandloi, Source: TokenDispatch, Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

Introduction

In a corner of crypto Twitter, a trader is watching a token that has surged 1400% in a week. This is another typical pump-and-dump scheme, but the project has almost no real users, and most of the tokens in circulation are lying in a few wallets that never transfer funds. He has seen this kind of trick too many times and knows how it will end.

So he makes the choice that most people would make: short, anticipating that the price may crash at any time, and then go to sleep. But when he wakes up the next morning, his short position has been liquidated.

The interesting point is that his judgment wasn't wrong. The price surge was indeed fake. He just didn't know what he was truly betting against. This isn't a fictional story. In September 2025, a token called MYX actually witnessed this. Without any major project announcements, product updates, new users, or on-chain activity, MYX surged from $1.30 to $18.42 in a week. Those who saw through the fake surge and chose to short it suffered a total loss of $89.51 million. These types of tokens are called "crime coins." The project team locks up the vast majority of the supply, colludes with trading institutions to manipulate the price, and then wipes out investors who try to go long or short. This article will dissect the operational model of such operations, the parties controlling the tokens, and why the industry has consistently turned a blind eye to it. All "crime coins" begin with a written agreement between two parties. One party is the project issuing the token, and the other party is the market maker—the trading institution that provides quotes on exchanges, enabling the tokens to trade. This step is largely the same across the crypto industry; the real difference lies in the contract details. These agreements typically stipulate a 70/30 split: for every $1 in profit generated from token trading, 70 cents go to the market maker, and 30 cents to the project team. Beyond profit sharing, the agreement also explicitly states that the market maker is responsible for "price management" of the token after its listing. This is euphemistically called "price guidance" in the industry, but in reality, it means the market maker is paid to manipulate the price in any direction during the week, according to the agreement. Reportedly, this partnership model has generated over $30 million in revenue per round for MYX and thousands of other tokens using the same approach. Crucially, the market makers acquire tokens through borrowing, not buying. This model is called the lending option model, and it's the standard paper form for cooperation between projects and market makers in the industry. When a token is listed, the project team lends a large number of tokens to the market maker, usually worth tens of millions of dollars. The market maker has 12 months to either return the tokens or buy them back at a pre-agreed higher price. This is just the written explanation; the actual operation is completely different: the market maker sells the borrowed tokens on the first day, using a massive supply to drive down the price; once the price drops to a low level, they buy back the same number of tokens at a lower price to return to the project team, pocketing the entire price difference. Criminal coins use this lending option model, the only change being the addition of a second profit channel. In addition to profiting from selling borrowed tokens to drive down the price, criminal coin operators have found a new method: to exploit retail traders who see through the manipulation and try to short the market to bet on a crash. From a technical standpoint, for this scheme to work, the project team and market makers must have near-total control over the circulating supply of tokens. And in the vast majority of fraudulent coin issuances, they have indeed achieved this. Typically, over 90% of the tokens are concentrated in wallet addresses traceable to the same operator. On block explorers, these addresses are split into a long list of seemingly unrelated accounts, creating the illusion of widespread holder distribution. However, on-chain analysis almost always confirms that these addresses are all controlled by the same group, and the splitting is merely to make it appear more legitimate. Take MYX as an example; the concentration of tokens is astonishingly high. At the peak of the price movement, only about 20% of the circulating supply was outside the control of the project team and its affiliates. The remaining 80% was concentrated in two places: one part was locked in an attribution contract, making it impossible to sell; the other part was stored in the wallets of core contributors and early investors, theoretically available for sale but chosen to remain inactive. In either case, these tokens never entered the market. Normally, this is the difference between a fully diluted token valuation and market capitalization: **Fully diluted valuation includes all tokens to be issued in the future, while market capitalization only counts currently circulating tokens.** However, in criminal cryptocurrencies, even this distinction becomes meaningless—because the tokens counted as circulating are not actually freely tradable. This is why MYX's market capitalization surged from $200 million to $3.35 billion in a week without any new user registrations or new fund deposits. The small fraction of MYX actually traded on exchanges sets a nominal price for a massive amount of tokens that remain completely untouched. A little scrutiny reveals a clear disconnect between MYX's price and its actual business. In the DeFi space, there's a standard metric called **TVL (Total Value Locked)**, which is simply the amount of money users deposit into a protocol to use the product. During the week MYX's market capitalization soared to $3.35 billion, its TVL remained stable between $25 million and $32 million, resulting in a market capitalization/TVL ratio close to 100. Even leading DeFi protocols like Uniswap and Aave have ratios between 1 and 4. The underlying fact is that, as a business entity, MYX's annual revenue is only about $5 million, yet it's valued at $17.7 billion, resulting in a price-to-sales ratio (P/S) of approximately 3500. This entire scheme had been in operation for a long time, long before the public saw the MYX price surge. Months before the price surge began, the manipulators conducted **fake trading** on multiple exchanges, including PancakeSwap, Bitget, and Binance. **Fake trading** refers to the same person simultaneously acting as both buyer and seller in a transaction, essentially buying from themselves to artificially inflate trading volume. This is because large exchanges heavily rely on trading volume data when listing and promoting tokens; artificially inflated trading volume on one exchange can often facilitate the successful listing of a token on another. Investigators, reviewing MYX's trading history, discovered that dozens of small buy orders from different exchanges ultimately converged into a single centralized wallet. A single operator artificially inflated demand by engaging in numerous small transactions with itself, creating the illusion of genuine market activity.

The Four Stages of Harvesting

The entire manipulation process is usually divided into four stages, each of which exploits the typical trading psychology of retail investors.

Stage 1: Luring Short Sellers

Market makers begin to drive up the token price with extremely low actual trading volume. This operation is very easy to achieve because most of the tokens have been locked up, and on smaller exchanges like Bitget and Gate, the order book itself is very thin.

When order book depth is insufficient, even a tiny trading volume can cause price fluctuations of 5% or even 10%. Take another criminal cryptocurrency, RAVE, as an example: even during periods of price manipulation with daily trading volume reaching billions of dollars, a one-way flow of only $172,000 on the Gate platform was enough to cause a 1% price fluctuation; a 10% increase would cost less than $2 million. The early price surges weren't aimed at profiting from the bullish trend. The real profit the manipulators were targeting came from the short sellers. The core objective of the first phase was to make the price surge appear obviously unsustainable, attracting skeptical traders to short the market. This is counterintuitive, because the more artificial the price surge appears, the more advantageous it is for the manipulators. Legitimate market makers aim for a natural, organic price increase, while those manipulating criminal coins deliberately create a manipulated price action to precisely attract short-selling traders. **Phase Two: Setting the Trap** Once a sufficient number of short positions have accumulated, the manipulators begin closing some of the long positions used for price manipulation, unwinding some hedging positions, and deliberately creating a noticeable pullback, while simultaneously reducing open interest significantly. When the price falls and open interest decreases simultaneously, the market appears to have formed a temporary top. This convinces short sellers on the sidelines that the rally has finally reversed, prompting them to enter the market and short during the pullback. **Phase Three: Short Squeeze and Crushing** This is the stage where short-selling traders begin to truly lose money. Most trading in criminal coins does not come from spot buying and holding, but rather through perpetual contracts. To balance long and short positions, exchanges charge a fee called **funding rate**. Every few hours, traders on one side of the market pay the other, depending on whether the perpetual contract price is higher or lower than the spot price: when the contract price is higher, long positions pay short positions; when the contract price is lower, short positions pay long positions. In a normal market, this rate is only a few tenths of a percent per day. However, in the short squeeze model of criminal coins, the funding rate for short positions reaches approximately -2% every 4 hours, equivalent to consuming 12% of the margin daily just for holding short positions. If held for a week, 84% of the funds would be exhausted before the price establishes a direction. Besides the funding rate, there is also leverage. The vast majority of short positions in criminal coins use 20x, 50x, and sometimes even 125x leverage. With 50x leverage, a 2% price reversal will result in a complete margin call, forcibly liquidating the position by the exchange. Once enough short positions are liquidated, a domino effect will occur: each liquidated short position will trigger forced buying, and each forced buying will push up the price; the higher price will then trigger another batch of short positions to be liquidated at even higher prices, forcing more passive buying into the market. Those traders who accurately judged the price surge as a scam will ultimately become the biggest buying force driving the price up, allowing the false upward trend to continue. MYX demonstrated the terrifying extent of this squeeze. On September 8, 2025, $16.53 million of MYX positions were liquidated in a single day, of which $13.68 million came from forced liquidation of short positions. In comparison, MYX's total open interest that day was approximately $262 million. This means that over 6% of the token's leveraged positions were wiped out within 24 hours, and almost all of them were short positions. Throughout the entire trading cycle, a total of $89.51 million in short positions were wiped out, while only $23.45 million in long positions were liquidated. The short liquidation was approximately four times the size of the long liquidation.

Phase Four: Distribution and Distribution

After the short sellers are wiped out and the price reaches a high point, the manipulator begins to reverse its position: opening its own short positions, closing its long positions, and gradually transferring the locked tokens to centralized exchanges. This step often traps many experienced on-chain traders. When tokens are transferred from the known manipulator's wallet to the exchange, the intuitive judgment is that a market crash is imminent, and the correct action should be to short.

... However, such transfers might be deliberately orchestrated, used as bait to lure a new wave of short sellers before the actual distribution begins. The core reason this entire operation works is the extremely singular counterparty. In the real market, participants have different judgments, holding periods, and information channels, but this is not the case in the criminal coin market. Whether going long or short, the ultimate counterparty in every transaction is the same operator executing the same set of tactics. Whoever controls the supply controls the market. All the manipulation tactics in criminal coins stem from one core fact: the project team and its affiliates almost entirely control the token supply. It is this absolute control that allows the same operator to simultaneously manipulate both spot and perpetual contracts for a particular token, deciding when the upward trend will stop and when short sellers will be squeezed out. Furthermore, no other party holds enough tokens to stop this. Taking MYX as an example, its price surged, with open interest even exceeding its market capitalization. Two-thirds of the trading volume was concentrated on Bitget, and short-selling funding rates remained at punitively high levels for several consecutive days. Traders attempting to short against the trend understood the market pattern but were completely unaware that it was all being manipulated. These traces were not deliberately hidden: the wallet clusters were traceable on-chain, the trading volume share was publicly visible on Coinglass, and market maker information was included in the project's fundraising plan. The entire industry turned a blind eye because everyone profited: market makers took a cut of the trading volume, project teams inflated the national treasury's valuation by controlling the circulating supply of their tokens, and exchanges earned fees regardless of the authenticity of the transactions. The only losers were retail investors.

data.title: Tesla earned $477 million in Q1; Musk complained while telling a story.
data.description:
Author: Su Yang, Tencent Technology

After the market closed on April 22 local time, Tesla released its first-quarter financial report, achieving a positive free cash flow of $1.44 billion and exceeding market expectations in terms of profit. The stock price subsequently rose by more than 4% in after-hours trading.

Data shows that Tesla's total revenue in the first quarter was $22.39 billion, a 16% increase from $19.3 billion in the same period last year. However, it was slightly lower than the $22.64 billion expected by analysts surveyed by LSEG. Tesla's profitability was outstanding in the first quarter. According to Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP), net income attributable to common shareholders reached $477 million, a 17% year-over-year increase; adjusted net income under non-GAAP was a staggering $1.453 billion, a 56% surge from $934 million in the same period last year. Adjusted earnings per share reached $0.41, exceeding Wall Street's expectation of $0.37. Behind these impressive financial results, Tesla is also undergoing a transformation—from a manufacturing company focused on car sales to a giant in the fields of artificial intelligence, autonomous driving, and humanoid robots. Its operating expenses surged 37% year-on-year to $3.78 billion, while capital expenditures soared 67% to $2.49 billion. Zacks Investment Research analyst Andrew Rocco believes this result "confirms that while the traditional electric vehicle business is no longer growing rapidly, it is stable enough to fund Tesla's massive investments in robotics and autonomous driving technology." Profit Engine Rebooted Tesla's total gross margin reached 21.1% in the quarter, an increase of 478 basis points from 16.3% in the same period last year. More noteworthy is the automotive business gross margin, which climbed to 19.2% excluding sales environment regulatory points, exceeding the level of any quarter last year. The significant improvement in profit margins is driven by multiple factors. Tesla explicitly stated in its financial report that higher average selling prices and "lower average cost per vehicle due to reduced material costs" jointly contributed to the improved profit margin. Meanwhile, "one-time gains" related to tariffs and vehicle warranties also contributed additional flexibility to the profit statement. Operating profit also showed a strong recovery momentum. Operating profit reached $941 million this quarter, a 136% increase from $399 million in the same period last year, with the operating profit margin improving from 2.1% to 4.2%. Adjusted EBITDA reached $3.668 billion, with a profit margin of 16.4%, an increase of 183 basis points year-over-year. However, this improvement in profitability is not without its concerns. Operating expenses surged 37% to $3.78 billion from $2.75 billion in the same period last year, clearly reflecting the company's rapidly expanding investment in artificial intelligence infrastructure, new product development, and capacity expansion. Hidden Concerns Behind Delivery Growth In the first quarter, Tesla delivered 358,023 vehicles globally, a 6% year-over-year increase, lower than Wall Street's expectation of 370,000 vehicles. However, considering the several weeks of lost production time due to Model Y production line upgrades in the same period last year, this performance still warrants careful interpretation. Wedbush Securities analyst Dan Ives called it a "disappointing start," but looking at a longer timeframe, cumulative deliveries have reached 9.2 million vehicles, a 21% year-over-year increase. Looking at the breakdown by model, the Model 3 and Model Y remain the absolute mainstays. The combined production of these two models in the first quarter was 394,611 units, with deliveries reaching 341,893 units, representing year-on-year increases of 14% and 6%, respectively. Meanwhile, production of "other models," including the Cybertruck, Model S, and Model X, decreased by 20% year-on-year to 13,775 units, while deliveries increased by 25% year-on-year to 16,130 units. This contrast is primarily due to Tesla's announcement in January that it would discontinue production of the Model S and Model X, and that its Fremont, California production line would be converted to manufacture the Optimus humanoid robot. The Cybertruck's situation is more delicate. Despite an annualized production capacity exceeding 125,000 vehicles, the market response to this aggressively styled electric pickup truck has been lukewarm. Tesla seems to have found a unique solution: selling the Cybertruck to another company under Musk's umbrella. Tesla confirmed in its financial report that the Cybercab and Tesla Semi are scheduled to enter mass production in 2026, and the first Cybercab was produced in February. Regarding production capacity, Tesla continues to optimize its global production network. The Fremont, California factory has an annual production capacity of over 550,000 Model 3 and Model Y vehicles, the Shanghai Gigafactory leads with a capacity of over 950,000 vehicles, the Berlin factory has a capacity of over 375,000 vehicles, and the Texas factory handles the production of the Model Y and Cybertruck. Tesla explicitly stated that it will prioritize fully utilizing and optimizing existing production capacity before building new factories and production lines. Tesla's Production Capacity Expansion for Different Products in Q1 2026 A worrying signal comes from inventory levels. At the end of Q1, global vehicle inventory days reached 27 days, up from 22 days in the same period last year, and significantly higher than the 15 days in the previous quarter. This means that despite continued delivery growth, the gap between production and sales is widening. In the first quarter, production reached 408,386 vehicles, while deliveries were only 358,023, a difference of over 50,000 vehicles. The energy storage business has cooled down. While energy generation and storage has been a consistently strong highlight in Tesla's financial reports in recent years, it experienced a rare decline in the first quarter. This segment generated $2.41 billion in revenue, a 12% decrease from $2.73 billion in the same period last year. Energy storage deployments totaled 8.8 gigawatt-hours, a 15% year-over-year decrease, a significant drop from the previous quarter's historical peak of 14.2 gigawatt-hours. However, this short-term fluctuation has not shaken Tesla's long-term commitment to the energy sector. The new Megafactory, located outside Houston, is progressing steadily. This factory will exclusively produce Megapack 3 solar panels for the Megablock and is scheduled to begin production later this year. In Shanghai, a Megapack factory with an annual capacity of 20 gigawatt-hours is still under construction. In the solar energy sector, Tesla has begun large-scale deployment of its first self-designed solar panels, produced at its New York Gigafactory. This new product features 18 independent power zones, three times that of traditional residential panels, enabling it to reliably generate more energy even in shaded conditions. Improved aesthetics and a faster, easier installation process are key differentiating factors for this product. Meanwhile, Tesla's Supercharger network continued to expand, adding over 2,200 charging points in the first quarter, bringing the total number of Supercharger stations globally to 8,463 and the number of charging points to 79,918, both representing a 19% year-over-year increase. FSD Subscriptions Surge If one were to extract the most telling metric from the first-quarter financial report reflecting Tesla's strategic focus, it would undoubtedly be the growth in FSD subscriptions. By the end of the first quarter, active FSD subscriptions reached 1.28 million, a 51% year-over-year surge, and a net increase of 180,000 from the previous quarter's 1.1 million, setting a new record for the highest single-quarter net increase. Tesla's Full Self-Driving (FSD) service has accumulated over 17.7 billion kilometers, with V12 and later versions contributing approximately 14.5 billion kilometers. Paid Robotaxi mileage nearly doubled quarter-over-quarter in the first quarter, exceeding 2.74 million kilometers. In April, Tesla further expanded its unsupervised operations area in Austin and officially launched unsupervised mobility services in Dallas and Houston. In the San Francisco Bay Area, ride-hailing services with safety drivers are also operating simultaneously. Tesla is preparing for service launches in Phoenix, Miami, Orlando, Tampa, and Las Vegas. The Dutch Vehicle Authority approved the deployment of FSD in the Netherlands in April, clearing the way for potential approvals in other EU countries. The Optimus humanoid robot is also one of Tesla's most imaginative future developments. Tesla announced that preparations for its first large-scale Optimus factory will begin in early Q2. The first-generation production line is designed to produce 1 million robots annually, directly replacing the existing Model S and Model X production lines at the Fremont factory. Meanwhile, the Texas Gigafactory is preparing for its second-generation production line, designed to produce 10 million robots annually in the long term. At the AI ​​infrastructure level, the Cortex 2 training cluster is officially online and running training tasks, with on-site training computing power continuing to climb. Tesla also confirmed that the next-generation AI5 inference processor completed its final chip design in April. Chip Factory and Soaring Capital Expenditure Tesla's capital expenditures reached $2.49 billion in the first quarter, a 67% surge from $1.49 billion in the same period last year. While this figure represents a significant increase, it is still only about half the quarterly average required for the full-year spending plan, which is one of the key reasons why Tesla was able to achieve positive free cash flow this quarter. Battery manufacturing is the top priority of capital investment. The lithium iron phosphate cell factory in Nevada, the cathode material factory in Texas, and the lithium refinery have all begun ramping up production. Specifically, the Nevada LFP capacity is 7 GWh, the Texas 4680 battery capacity is 40 GWh, the cathode material capacity is 10 GWh, and the lithium refinery capacity is 30 GWh, with the latter three all in the early stages of ramping up. The expansion of AI training computing power has also consumed a significant amount of capital. The Cortex 1 cluster in Texas boasts over 100,000 H100-equivalent GPUs, while the Cortex 2 cluster is equipped with over 130,000 H100-equivalent GPUs and is in the early stages of upgrades. According to Tesla's published training capacity upgrade curve, the total capacity, both existing and planned, has grown from near zero to over 300,000 H100-equivalent GPUs. Development of the Dojo 3 custom chip continues, with the goal of reducing long-term training costs. Tesla's Computing Cluster Expansion in Q1 2026 The TeraFab chip factory plan is given special strategic significance. Tesla describes this project as "the largest chip factory ever," aiming to vertically integrate logic, memory, and advanced packaging capabilities to enable rapid iteration when chip demand exceeds industry capacity. This is not only a measure to ensure chip supply security, but also potentially opens up a completely new business dimension for Tesla, aiming to manufacture chips for robotics, artificial intelligence, and space data centers. The path of "trading current profits for a future story" has two explicit costs: a shrinking profit margin and, more implicitly, volatility in the core automotive business. Despite year-over-year growth in deliveries this quarter, the Wall Street Journal points out that this is still Tesla's second-worst quarterly sales since 2022. Meanwhile, the market's valuation of the "future story" is already overvalued. Bloomberg analysis reminds investors that Tesla, with a forward P/E ratio of 183, ranks as the third most expensive stock in the S&P 500, far exceeding other tech giants among the "Big Seven" U.S. stocks. The following is a condensed version of Tesla's Q1 2026 earnings call with analysts: Tesla CEO Elon Musk provides commentary: 2026 will be very exciting. Tesla will significantly increase capital expenditures, investing heavily in batteries, AI training, chip design, and the supply chain. New products (Cybercab, Semi, Optimus Prime) will all experience a prolonged S-curve, with slow initial production increases but exponential growth from the end of this year to next year. Demand for Megapack is strong, and a new factory near Houston will begin production later this year. Regarding FSD, V14.3 is a major architectural update that promises to allow unsupervised FSD to operate legally in all regions globally. V15 will be launched by the end of this year or early next year, with safety far exceeding that of humans. Autonomous taxis have expanded to Dallas and Houston; the only limitation to this expansion is rigorous safety verification, and the team currently maintains a zero-accident record. Optimus Prime V3 is expected to be showcased by mid-year. We don't really want to showcase it too early because competitors will copy it frame by frame. Internal production will begin later this year at the Fremont factory, with significant volume increases next year, and the second Texas factory will begin production next summer. Optimus Prime will be the most important product in Tesla's and even the world's history. Technically, AI5 has been taped out and is the most cost-effective edge AI inference chip. AI6 and Dojo 3 are under development. A chip research factory will be built at the Texas Giga campus, with construction starting this year. We are simultaneously advancing multiple extremely challenging projects. This is exactly what the team excels at—solving the most difficult problems and creating amazing products. The following is an analyst Q&A session: Q: When can we expect to see Optimus Prime 3 unveiled? When will Optimus Prime production begin after Model X/S production ceases? What will be the production rate and initial target size by the end of this year? Musk: We want to postpone the Optimus Prime 3 launch until closer to production because competitors copy every demonstration frame by frame. Production will begin around the end of July to the beginning of August. The last batch of Model S/X production took place in early May. After the final assembly line was completed, it was dismantled. Dismantling and reinstalling the new line takes at least several months. Completing dismantling, reassembling, and starting within four months is extremely fast; almost no other company on Earth has achieved that. This year's production rate is unpredictable, given that this is a completely new product, a completely new production line, and tens of thousands of unique projects. The ramp-up speed depends on the slowest link. It will be very slow at first; we need to start by gradually building up the simplest skills in the factory. Q: Besides Austin, what are the target milestones for the expansion of unsupervised FSD and autonomous taxis this year? How will this drive recurring revenue growth? Musk: We hope to be operational in about a dozen states by the end of this year. We're being very cautious with the rollout, with zero injuries and zero fatalities to date, and we hope to maintain that record. Revenue may not grow significantly this year, but it will accelerate next year. Q: When will unsupervised FSD be rolled out to customer vehicles? Musk: Probably in the fourth quarter of this year. We won't be rolling it out to everyone, everywhere at once. We need to make sure each city doesn't have particularly complex intersections, bad road markings, or weather challenges. We'll roll it out gradually after confirming safety. Q: How can a Hardware 3 car achieve unsupervised FSD? Musk: Hardware 3 can't. Its memory bandwidth is only 1/8 that of Hardware 4, and memory bandwidth is a key bottleneck for unsupervised FSD. Customers who have already purchased FSD can trade in their old vehicles for a discount to an AI4 vehicle, or upgrade their computers and cameras to Hardware 4, but this requires establishing micro-factories in major metropolitan areas to operate efficiently, as doing it only at service centers would be extremely slow. In the long term, it makes sense to convert all Hardware 3 vehicles to Hardware 4 so they can join the autonomous taxi fleet. In the meantime, we will release a distilled version of Hardware 3, V14, at the end of June, with all the features of Hardware 4 V14, capable of starting from a parked state. Q: What allowed you to complete the AI5 tape-out ahead of schedule? What changes were made to the initial vision? Has AI5 been removed from the roadmap? Musk: Our team worked incredibly hard, every weekend and holiday for six consecutive months, without making any major mistakes. AI5 will be used in Optimus Prime and data centers because AI4 already achieves unsupervised autonomous driving levels far exceeding human safety, so AI5 isn't immediately needed in cars. However, switching to AI5 at some point in the future makes sense. We also plan to launch AI4+ (production by mid-next year), increasing RAM from 16GB to 32GB, with computing power and bandwidth increased by approximately 10%. Q: Is V14.3 the final piece of the puzzle for achieving large-scale unsupervised FSD, or must we wait until V15? Musk: 14.3 is the final piece of the puzzle. The issue lies in the degree of security and convenience. We know that major architectural improvements will significantly increase the security probability. When we know there are software improvements that can improve security, there's no point in deploying unsupervised FSD on a large scale; instead, we should complete the writing, verification, and then release it. Currently, the self-driving taxis operating in Austin, Dallas, and Houston are all running a variant of 14.3, which is obviously safer. We will continue to expand on V14.3 for some time, and V15 will be a major upgrade. Question: What are the respective responsibilities of the parties involved in the Terafab project (funding, design, construction, operation, production, etc.)? Is Intel involved? Musk: The details are still being finalized. In the short term, Tesla is building a research wafer fab at its Gigafactory in Texas, costing approximately $3 billion and with a monthly capacity of several thousand wafers. The aim is to test new ideas and physical principles, and verify production reliability. SpaceX will handle the initial phase of the expanded Terafab. ​​Any internal company matters require approval from both boards of directors and conflict resolution procedures to ensure a balance of interests between Tesla and SpaceX shareholders, all of which take time. Intel will collaborate on core manufacturing technologies; we plan to use Intel's 14A process (the most advanced, but not yet fully completed), which should be mature by the time Terafab scales up. We have a good relationship with Intel. The research wafer fab will house masks, logic, memory, and packaging in the same building, enabling the fastest recursive development and experimenting with radical ideas. While most are unlikely to succeed, if successful, they will fundamentally improve the way chips work. Q: How does the Optimus Prime architecture integrate with xAI and Grok? Is System 2 intelligence implemented on the chip? Will millions of Optimus Prime units per year create inference demand for data centers? Musk: We can put a lot of intelligence locally on the robot, so it won't freeze even if the connection is lost, just like a car can drive safely without a network. Optimus Prime needs a manager (orchestration AI) to tell it what to do, and Grok is well-suited for orchestration. Regarding voice, Grok is a low-latency intelligent voice AI capable of Grok-level conversations. Optimus Prime could potentially work for hours without any management supervision. Q: To what extent is Terafab aimed at achieving better chip economics? How long will it take to reach economic parity yield? Musk: Terafab is not meant to exert influence over chip suppliers. As we scale up, industry growth (especially memory) cannot provide enough AI chips, and we'll hit a wall if we don't make them ourselves. We have some research ideas about making better chips (unlikely to succeed, but if successful, it would be a huge leap forward). In the long run, for example, chips for AI satellites, the existing industry simply cannot keep up. Q: Has your view on the new car model changed? Is Cybercab a compact car? When will the Roadster be unveiled? Musk: Cybercab is a compact car, very spacious, but it's a two-seater. 90% of the driving will be done by one or two people, so the majority of production in the long run should be Cybercab. But the entire product line should eventually consist of autonomous vehicles of different sizes. The only manually driven car in the long run will be the new Tesla Roadster. The Roadster will likely be first showcased in about a month, requiring extensive testing and validation. It will be one of the most exciting product launches ever, and possibly one of the most spectacular demos ever.

Q: After removing the safety driver, what key safety metrics (miles per intervention, mileage per accident, mileage per death) are you tracking to confirm scalability? Where are you currently?

Musk: We track all the metrics mentioned. We have a fairly large QA fleet across the US, looking at any interventions and simulating them using neural networks in simulators. All expansions are performing as expected. Many factors limiting wider deployment are not safety issues, but convenience issues—the car becomes paranoid and gets stuck because it's programmed to prioritize safety to the maximum extent, sometimes afraid to do things. For example, afraid to cross a railway track, stuck at a red light that never turns green, or endlessly looping around when there's an accident or construction. These issues are more complex than direct safety concerns.

data.title: The person who brought Web3 closest to AI
data.description:
Author: Zhou, ChainCatcher

On April 22, SpaceX announced a partnership with AI programming tool Cursor, acquiring the rights to acquire it for $60 billion. This valuation of Cursor has more than doubled from approximately $29.3 billion last November.

This news has reignited discussion about a name that had gradually faded from view—SBF.

In 2022, Alameda Research, a subsidiary of SBF, invested $200,000 in Anysphere, the parent company of Cursor, acquiring approximately 5% of its shares. After FTX's bankruptcy liquidation, this stake was disposed of at the original price. Based on the current valuation of $60 billion, if held until now, it would be worth approximately $3 billion. Over the past year, the valuations of AI giants such as Anthropic and OpenAI have repeatedly reached new highs, and the AI ​​sector continues to heat up. In the AI ​​field, Cursor is just a small investment among SBF's many bets; the largest is Anthropic. Currently, Anthropic is in negotiations for a new round of financing, with some VC offers corresponding to valuations as high as $800 billion. In February of this year, the company completed a financing round with a valuation of $380 billion; data from the secondary market platform Caplight shows that its outstanding shares have risen to a valuation of approximately $688 billion. SBF previously led Anthropic's Series B funding round with $500 million, holding approximately 8% of the shares. Based on the current valuation of $380 billion, this investment, if not liquidated, would be worth over $30 billion. However, the liquidation team sold in batches that year, ultimately realizing only $1.4 billion. Time has proven that SBF may be one of the most successful AI investors in the crypto space. In 2021, the AI ​​boom had not yet arrived, ChatGPT was still a year away from release, and Anthropic had just spun off from OpenAI; very few people were willing to bet $500 million on this startup. Cursor was virtually unknown at the time. Beyond AI, his foresight was equally ahead of the market: when Solana was still a niche project, he bought in at an average price of $8, reaching a peak unrealized profit of approximately $2.1 billion; his 7.5% stake in Robinhood is currently worth about $10 billion; and his $100 million investment in Mysten Labs has now appreciated by over $800 million. If these assets had not been confiscated, their total value would likely be in the tens of billions of dollars. SBF was a big winner of that era, his net worth once exceeding $16 billion, earning him a place on the Forbes rich list and making him a guest of honor for Washington politicians. Even today, in 2026, his name is still frequently mentioned. Especially in the current crypto world, an inexplicable sense of loss is spreading. Frequent hacker attacks, AAVE's bad debt crisis with $15.1 billion flowing out in three and a half days, and the temporary locking of ordinary users' assets; the prices of DeFi blue-chip tokens such as UNI, AAVE, MKR, and CRV are severely decoupled from their fundamentals. Veteran players lament, "This time, it's different from before." The grand narratives are collapsing one after another—DeFi, NFTs, blockchain games… with each wave receding, only more fatigue and less trust remain. The narrative's period of opportunity is visibly shrinking, from two years to one year, then to just a few months; fewer and fewer people are able to capitalize on the first wave. Early opportunities are also narrowing. On the one hand, on-chain data is highly transparent, and the movements of smart money are almost trackable in real time. The information gap window has narrowed dramatically, and VCs have already locked up low-priced tokens, leaving retail investors with tokens valued at dozens of times their initial investment during TGE. On the other hand, cases of market makers colluding with project teams to manipulate token prices are commonplace, and the credibility of price signals themselves is declining. Even the hype surrounding Memecoin is fading, and crypto VCs are generally turning to low-risk arbitrage, making the entire industry "boring." The industry's sense of loss is evident. But are people really missing SBF himself? When FTX collapsed, approximately one million creditors suffered heavy losses, and ordinary depositors' assets were wiped out overnight. SBF misappropriated customer funds and falsified balance sheets, ultimately being sentenced to 25 years in prison for fraud. It left behind not only a wealth myth, but also countless bank statements showing people lost everything. SBF was the most extreme symbol of its time, and the collapse of FTX, to some extent, accelerated the end of that era. What people truly miss is that unique sense of energy—major news and breakthroughs every day, opportunities for ordinary people, rules yet to be written, blank spaces everywhere, crypto hype surpassing AI, VC still a whale-hunting game, risks and rewards coexisting, and opportunities rife with unpriced opportunities. However, that era where most people could get a share of the pie is over. Yet, the crypto industry is far from extinct. As old narratives crumble, new ones are quietly emerging, especially at the intersection of AI and crypto, which is far from being fully priced in. Binance founder Changpeng Zhao (CZ) recently pointed out in a Binance Square AMA that while AI is currently attracting significant funding and attention, this has actually created more long-term builders for the crypto industry, an environment beneficial for its long-term development. Several VC executives also reached a consensus in their 2026 outlook: "The spray-and-pray era is over," and 2026 will be the year where "Execution, Not Hype" determines success. Capital is shifting from chasing trends to supporting builders with real business models, strong execution capabilities, and long-term patience. As DWF Labs co-founder Andrei Grachev stated, the market is currently in a "very boring" phase, but it's not dying out. There's still plenty to do as a builder or investor. Small investors should maintain a learning mindset, avoid getting caught up in the frenzy, and enjoy the market they've chosen to enter.

data.title: a16z: Why Large Models Need Continuous Learning in the AI ​​Era
data.description:
Authors: Malika Aubakirova, Matt Bornstein; Source: a16z; Compiled by: Shaw, Jinse Finance

In Christopher Nolan's film *Memento*, Leonard Shelby lives in a fragmented present. After suffering a traumatic brain injury, he develops anterograde amnesia, unable to form new memories. Every few minutes, his world resets, trapping him in an eternal present, disconnected from what has just happened and uncertain about what will happen next. To survive, he gets cue tattoos and takes Polaroid photos, using these external tools to remember information his brain cannot retain.

Large Language Models (LLMs) also live in a similar eternal present.

Large Language Models (LLMs) These models acquire massive amounts of knowledge during training, which is then solidified in their parameters, but they cannot form new memories—they cannot update their parameters based on new experiences. To compensate for this deficiency, we have built various auxiliary frameworks for them: treating dialogue history as short-term notes, the retrieval system as an external notebook, and system prompts as guiding tattoos. But the model itself never truly internalizes new information. A growing number of researchers believe this is far from sufficient. Contextual learning (ICL) is adequate for problems where the answer or fragments of the answer already exist somewhere in the world. But for problems requiring truly original discovery (such as entirely new mathematical problems), adversarial scenarios (such as cybersecurity), or tacit knowledge that is difficult to express in language, there are strong reasons why models need the ability to directly update their parameters with knowledge and experience after deployment. Contextual learning is fleeting. True learning requires information compression. If we cannot allow models to continuously engage in compressed learning, we may be forever trapped in a perpetual present moment, like fragments of memory. Conversely, if we can enable models to learn to build their own memory architectures, rather than relying on customized external tools, we might unlock entirely new dimensions of scalable upgrades. This research area is known as **continuous learning**. Although the concept is not new (dating back to McCloskey and Cohen's 1989 research), we believe it is one of the most important research directions in the field of artificial intelligence today. In the past two or three years, model capabilities have seen astonishing growth, and the gap between what a model "knows" and what it "can know" has become increasingly apparent. Therefore, this article aims to share insights gained from our discussions with leading researchers in this field, clarifying different technical paths for continuous learning, and promoting the development of this topic within the startup ecosystem. Let's talk about context first. Before arguing about parametric learning (i.e., learning achieved by updating model weights), it must be acknowledged that contextual learning is indeed effective, and there are good reasons to believe it will continue to dominate. The Transformer is essentially a next-word prediction model that conditions a sequence. Given a suitable input sequence, it can exhibit surprisingly rich behavior without altering the weights. This is why context management, cue word engineering, instruction fine-tuning, and few-shot learning are so powerful. Intelligence is inherent in static parameters, while the model's outward performance changes drastically with the content of the input window. Cursor's recent in-depth analysis of scaling autonomous programmable agents illustrates this well: "A large part of the system's performance ultimately depends on how we design cue words for the agent. Frameworks and models are important, but cue words are even more crucial." Model weights are fixed. What truly makes the system work is the fine-tuning of the context: what information to include, when to summarize, and how to maintain consistency during hours of autonomous operation. OpenClaw is another excellent example. What sets it apart isn't special model access (the underlying model is open to everyone), but rather its ability to efficiently transform context and tools into a workable state: tracking your actions, structuring intermediate products, determining what needs to be re-injected with prompts, and retaining a persistent memory of past work. OpenClaw elevates agent framework design to an independent specialty. When prompts first appeared, many researchers doubted whether "prompts alone" could become a formal interaction method; at the time, it seemed more like a shortcut. However, this approach is naturally suited to the Transformer architecture, requiring no retraining and automatically scaling as model performance improves. Therefore, the more powerful the model, the better the prompts. "Simple but native" interaction methods often prevail because they directly collaborate with the underlying system, rather than fighting against it. This is currently the case in the field of large language models. State Space Model: A Powerful Version of Contextual Capabilities As mainstream workflows shift from directly calling large language models to agent loops, the pressure on context learning models is constantly increasing. Previously, situations where the context was completely filled were relatively rare, usually only occurring when the model performed a long series of independent tasks, and the application layer could easily reduce or compress the dialogue history. However, in agent scenarios, a single task can consume a large amount of available context space. Each step in an agent loop depends on the context passed from the previous round, often failing after 20 to 100 steps—because the context is exhausted, logical coherence decreases, and convergence is ultimately impossible. Therefore, major AI labs are now investing heavily in (such as large-scale training tasks) to develop models with ultra-large context windows. This is a logical approach, as it is based on proven effective context learning and perfectly aligns with the industry's shift towards inference computing power. The most common architecture alternates between fixed-memory layers and regular attention heads, namely the state-space model and various linear attention variants (for simplicity, we collectively refer to them as SSM). When dealing with long contexts, the scalability of SSM is fundamentally superior to traditional attention mechanisms. Its goal is to help agents maintain logical coherence in longer loops, increasing the effective number of steps from approximately 20 to approximately 20,000, without sacrificing the extensive skills and knowledge possessed by traditional Transformers. If this approach proves feasible, it will be a major breakthrough for long-running agents. You could even view this as a form of continuous learning: while the model weights aren't updated, an external memory layer is introduced that requires almost no resetting. Therefore, these non-parametric methods are real and powerful. Any evaluation of continuous learning must begin here. The question isn't whether current context-based systems are effective—they are. The question is whether we've reached our limits and whether new methods can take us further. Limitations of Context: The Filing Cabinet Fallacy "What happens with AGI and pre-training is that, in a sense, they exceed their goals… Humans are not AGI. Admittedly, humans have a basic skill set, but lack a vast amount of knowledge. Instead, we rely on continuous learning. If I create a super-intelligent 15-year-old, he actually knows very little. He is an excellent student, full of curiosity. You can tell him: 'Become a programmer, become a doctor.' Model deployment itself requires a learning and trial-and-error process. It is a gradual process, not a direct delivery of a finished product." — Ilya Sutskevi Imagine a system with unlimited storage space: the world's largest filing cabinet, where every fact is perfectly indexed and instantly retrieved. It can find any information. But has it learned? No. It was never required to compress information. This is key to our core argument, drawing on the viewpoint of Ilya Sutskevi: the essence of large language models is compression algorithms. During training, they compress the internet into their parameters. This compression is lossy, and that's precisely its strength. Compression forces the model to discover structure, achieve generalization, and build representations that can be transferred across contexts. Models that merely memorize training samples are far inferior to those that can extract underlying patterns. Lossy compression itself is learning. The irony is that this very mechanism that makes large language models powerful during training (compressing raw data into compact, transferable representations) is something we refuse to use after model deployment. We stop compression when the model is released, replacing it with external memory. Of course, most agent frameworks will compress context in some customized way. But given these "painful lessons," shouldn't the models themselves learn this compression directly and on a large scale? Yu Sun shared a mathematical example to illustrate this debate. Take Fermat's Last Theorem: for over 350 years, no mathematician could prove it—not because of a lack of literature, but because the solution was extremely innovative. The conceptual gap between the existing mathematical system and the final answer was simply too vast. In the 1990s, British mathematician Andrew Wiles, after seven years of almost isolated research, finally cracked this problem, having to create a powerful new method to complete the proof. His proof successfully bridged two major branches of mathematics—elliptic curves and modular forms. Although American mathematician Ken Ribett's previous research had proven that proving this connection would solve Fermat's Last Theorem, before Wiles, no one possessed the theoretical tools to build this bridge. The same logic applies to the proof of the Poincaré conjecture by Russian mathematician Grigory Perelman. The core question is: do these examples prove that large language models lack a certain ability—the ability to update prior knowledge and engage in truly creative thinking? Or do these examples actually prove the opposite conclusion: that all human knowledge is merely data available for training and reorganization, and that Wiles and Perelman's achievements are simply the effect that large language models can achieve on a larger scale? This is a question that requires empirical testing and remains inconclusive. However, we have clearly identified many types of problems that current contextual learning cannot solve, while parametric learning may be able to play a role. For example:

Furthermore, contextual learning is limited to content that can be expressed in language, while model weights can encode concepts that cannot be conveyed through cues in words. Some patterns are too high-dimensional, too implicit, or too deeply structured to be contained in context. For example, the visual texture that distinguishes benign lesions from tumors in medical images, or the subtle audio fluctuations that define a speaker's unique rhythm, are difficult to break down into precise textual descriptions. Language can only approximate them. No matter how long the cues are, they cannot convey this kind of knowledge—this kind of knowledge can only exist in the weights. They exist in the latent space of the learned representation, not in the words.

... No matter how large the context window expands, there will always be knowledge that cannot be described in text and can only be stored in parameters. This might explain why explicit "robot remembers you" features like ChatGPT memory often make users uncomfortable rather than delighted. Users don't actually want simple recollection; they want capability. A model that internalizes your behavioral patterns can generalize to handle new scenarios; a model that only retrieves your past records cannot. The difference between "This is your previous reply to this email" (word-for-word paraphrasing) and "I understand your thought process well enough to anticipate your needs" is the essential difference between retrieval and learning. Continuous Learning Introduction Continuous learning has multiple implementation paths; the core dividing line is not "whether or not there is a memory function," but rather: where does compression occur? The relevant methods can be roughly divided into three categories, forming a continuous spectrum: Uncompressed (pure retrieval, weight freezing) Fully internally compressed (weight-level learning, the model truly becomes smarter) An important intermediate solution: Modular solution On the context side, the research team will build a more intelligent retrieval process, agent framework, and prompt word arrangement system. This is currently the most mature direction: the infrastructure has been validated, and the deployment process is clear and controllable. Its limitation lies in depth, that is, the length of the context. Here is an emerging extension direction worth paying attention to: multi-agent architecture, which serves as an extension strategy for the context itself. If a single model is limited to a 128K token window, then a cluster of collaborative agents—each agent holding its own context, specializing in a specific part of the problem, and sharing results—can achieve approximately infinite working memory as a whole. Each agent completes context learning within its own window, and then the system aggregates the results. Karpathy's recent independent research project and Cursor's case of building a web browser are examples of this early practice. This is a purely non-parametric method (without changing the weights), yet it greatly increases the upper limit that context-based systems can achieve. Modularization: In terms of modularization, the team builds mountable knowledge modules (compressed key-value caches, adapter layers, external repositories, etc.) to enable general-purpose models to possess specialized capabilities without retraining. An 8 billion-parameter model, paired with suitable modules, can achieve the performance of a 109 billion-parameter model on a specific task, while consuming very little memory. The appeal of this approach lies in its compatibility with existing Transformer architectures. Weight Updates: In terms of weight updates, researchers are exploring true parameterized learning, such as sparse memory layers that update only relevant parameters, reinforcement learning loops that continuously optimize the model through feedback, and test-time training that compresses context into weights during the inference phase. These are the most advanced solutions and also the most difficult to deploy, but they truly allow the model to fully internalize new information or skills. Currently, there are several parameter update mechanisms. The following are some representative research directions: The research field at the weight level encompasses multiple parallel technical routes. **Regularization and weight space methods** are the oldest approach: **Elastic weight fixation** penalizes parameter changes based on their importance to previous tasks; **Weight interpolation** fuses new and old weight combinations in the parameter space. However, both methods often lack stability in large-scale applications. **Training during Test Time (TTT)**, first proposed by Sun et al. in 2020, has since evolved into various basic architectural modules (TTT layers, end-to-end TTT, TTT-Discover, etc.), each with a distinct approach: performing gradient descent on test data and compressing new information into model parameters at key nodes. Meta-learning explores the possibility of training models with learning capabilities, ranging from MAML parameter initialization methods suitable for small sample scenarios to nested learning proposed by Behrouz et al. in 2025. The latter designs the model as a multi-level optimization structure, running at different time scales, and, inspired by biological memory consolidation mechanisms, incorporates fast adaptation and slow update modules. **Knowledge distillation** preserves old task knowledge by having the student model fit frozen teacher model weights. LoRD achieves efficient and continuous operation by simultaneously pruning the model and replay buffer. **Self-distillation** reverses the signal source, using the model's own output generated based on expert conditions as the training signal, avoiding the catastrophic forgetting problem common in continuous fine-tuning. The concept of recursive self-evolution is similar: STaR achieves iterative capability through model-generated inference logic; AlphaEvolve discovered algorithmic optimization directions that hadn't been improved for decades; Silver and Sutton's "Era of Experience" builds agent learning on a continuous flow of experience. These research directions are gradually converging. TTT-Discover has combined test-time training with reinforcement learning-driven exploration; the HOPE architecture nests fast and slow dual learning loops within a single model; SDFT transforms distillation into a self-improving foundational unit. The boundaries between different technical routes are becoming increasingly blurred—the next generation of continuous learning systems is likely to integrate multiple strategies, using regularization to ensure stability, meta-learning to accelerate learning, and self-improvement to achieve compound interest in capabilities. More and more startups are developing strategies around different aspects of this technical system. The startup ecosystem in the continuous learning field is currently dominated by non-parametric approaches. Intelligent agent framework vendors (Letta, mem0, Subconscious) build orchestration layers and auxiliary architectures to manage the content of the input context window; external storage and retrieval augmented generation (RAG) infrastructures (such as Pinecone, xmemory) provide underlying retrieval support. The data itself already exists; the core challenge lies in how to filter and provide accurate data fragments to the model at the right time. As the context window continues to expand, the design space for these companies also expands, especially in the framework field, where a number of startups are emerging to manage increasingly complex context strategies. The parameterization route is at an earlier stage of development and has more diverse technical approaches. Related companies are attempting to implement various post-deployment compression solutions, allowing the model to internalize new information in the weights. Based on the learning method after model deployment, these paths can be divided into several clear directions. Local compression: learning without retraining. Some teams are developing mountable knowledge modules (compressed key-value caches, adapter layers, external memory libraries) to endow general-purpose models with specialized capabilities without changing the core weights. Its core concept is to achieve meaningful information compression, rather than just retrieval, under a controllable balance of stability and plasticity, because the learning process is isolated rather than scattered throughout the parameter space. An 8 billion parameter model, paired with appropriate modules, can achieve performance far exceeding that of larger models on specific tasks. The advantage of this approach lies in its composability: modules can be directly adapted to existing Transformer architectures, can be independently replaced or updated, and the experimental cost is far lower than retraining. Reinforcement Learning and Feedback Loop: Learning from Signals. Other teams believe that the richest signals for post-deployment learning already exist within the deployment process—user corrections, task success or failure, and reward feedback from real-world scenarios. The core idea is that the model should treat every interaction as a potential training signal, rather than just an inference request. This is highly similar to the pattern of human skill development at work: practice, receiving feedback, and internalizing effective experience. The engineering challenge lies in transforming sparse, noisy, and even adversarial feedback into stable weight updates, avoiding catastrophic forgetting. Once the model can truly learn from deployment, its value accumulates over time, something a purely context-based system cannot achieve. A data-centric approach: Learning from high-quality signals. A related but independent direction argues that the bottleneck is not the learning algorithm, but the training data and supporting systems. These teams focus on filtering, generating, or synthesizing high-quality data to drive continuous updates. Their core premise is that if the model can acquire high-quality, structured learning signals, effective improvement can be achieved with fewer gradient steps. This aligns with the feedback loop approach, but emphasizes upstream issues: focusing not only on whether the model can learn, but also on the content and extent of learning. A completely new architecture: Achieving learning from the design level. The most radical approach argues that the Transformer architecture itself is the bottleneck, and continuous learning requires entirely different underlying computing units—an architecture with continuous-time dynamic characteristics and built-in memory mechanisms. Its core concept is structural: to build a continuous learning system, the learning mechanism should be built into the infrastructure. Major mainstream laboratories are also actively investing in these areas. Some are exploring better context management and thought chain reasoning solutions, while others are experimenting with external memory modules or offline computing (sleep-period computing) processes. Several undisclosed startups are also developing entirely new architectures. This field is still in its early stages, with no single method holding a dominant position; and given the diversity of application scenarios, a single dominant player is unlikely in the future.
